card.intuition: Multiplying a chain of matrices A₁A₂...Aₙ — parenthesisation order doesn't change the result but dramatically changes the cost. dp[i][j] = minimum scalar multiplications to multiply matrices i..j. Try every split point k: dp[i][j] = min over k of (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + rows[i]*cols[k]*cols[j]).
card.analogy: Deciding the order to process a pipeline of transforms: doing a cheap operation early can drastically reduce the size of data flowing through the rest, making subsequent operations cheaper.
when_to_use: Optimising matrix multiplication order in linear algebra pipelines
Parsing with CYK algorithm (same DP structure)
Interval DP template: any problem of the form 'optimal way to split a range'
gotchas: Input is dimensions array where matrix i has dims[i] × dims[i+1] — length n+1 for n matrices
dp[i][i] = 0 for all i (single matrix, no multiplication needed)
Fill by chain length l = 2, 3, ..., n — not by row/column

exercise.starter_code:
def matrix_chain(dims):
    """
    dims: list of n+1 dimensions; matrix i has shape dims[i] x dims[i+1]
    Returns minimum number of scalar multiplications.
    """
    n = len(dims) - 1
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]

    for length in range(2, n + 1):          # chain length
        for i in range(n - length + 1):
            j = i + length - 1
            dp[i][j] = float('inf')
            for k in range(i, j):           # split point
                cost = (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]
                        + ___________)
                dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], cost)
    return dp[0][n-1]
hints: The cost of multiplying the left and right parts together is dims[i] * dims[k+1] * dims[j+1] — rows of left part × inner dimension × columns of right part
exercise.test_code:
# 4 matrices: 10x30, 30x5, 5x60 -> min = 27000
assert matrix_chain([10,30,5,60]) == 27000
# Classic: 40x20, 20x30, 30x10, 10x30
assert matrix_chain([40,20,30,10,30]) == 26000
# Single matrix
assert matrix_chain([5,10]) == 0
# Two matrices
assert matrix_chain([2,3,4]) == 24  # 2*3*4 = 24
print('PASS')
